03
03 [ Red team — offensive ops ]

Projects & Offensive Path

Independent work — breaking things down to understand how they fail.

OP // LOCAL_LLM

Llama 3 (8B) local deployment

Ran the Llama 3 (8B) model locally on my own laptop using Ollama. When inference crawled, I opened Task Manager and traced it live: RAM climbing from 38% idle to 90% under load — the model consuming nearly all 16 GB. Hands-on proof of how much hardware matters when running AI locally.
> deploy → observe → diagnose
OP // ILWW_COACH

ILWW Coach — Garmin-powered PWA

An installable, offline-capable running coach I directed end-to-end and built with Claude Code. A scheduled Python job logs into my own Garmin account and recomputes ~50 metrics that Garmin and Strava paywall — readiness, execution grades, adaptive race plans — into one JSON file a dependency-free vanilla-JS app renders. No framework, no server; personal state never leaves the device.
> garmin → python action → PWA · ~2,300 lines, zero dependencies
launch the live app ↗
OP // LAB_GRIND

07
07 [ System — build log ]

How This Was Built

Full transparency — this portfolio is itself one of my projects, and this is how I ran it.

STAGE 1 // DIRECTION

My story, my call

The concept, the red team / blue team structure, and every fact on this page came from me — my certificates, my screenshots, my writeups. Nothing here was invented for effect.
STAGE 2 // SOURCING

Research & raw material

I pulled UI/UX design-system repos from GitHub as reference material — and kept feeding in new repos, libraries and assets as the build grew — while holding the brief steady: dark SOC-console theme, terminal everywhere, no generic template look.
STAGE 3 // AI TOOLING

Built with AI, on my prompts

The visual system was designed in Claude Design and the code generated with Claude Code — feature by feature, driven by my prompts across 10+ revision rounds. Knowing how to direct AI tools properly is a skill I'm bringing to this course.
STAGE 4 // REVIEW

Checked by me, every build

I tested each version, caught what felt wrong — pacing, labels, claims — and sent it back until it was right. Trust, but verify: it applies to AI output too.
STAGE 5 // DEPLOYMENT

Shipped by me

Hosted on my own GitHub: I set up the repos, uploaded the builds, and stood this site up on GitHub Pages myself — including debugging my first deploy's 404 (a rogue index (1).html; every dev earns that badge eventually).

// nothing load-bearing was generated: the certificates, dates, screenshots and stats are all real — and all verifiable above.

thanks for reading all the way — jadon
